race.name: BWR Montana
race.tagline: Montana Rockies adventure—AKA 'Hell of the Bozone': mountain gravel around Bozeman, waffles at the finish.

vitals.date_specific: 2026: June 19-20 (Friday check-in/expo, Saturday race day — CONFIRMED, belgianwaffleride.bike; 2027 not yet announced)
terrain_types: Montana Rockies gravel (~60% unpaved on the Waffle course)
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple on course (reported at roughly miles 22, 39, 67, 98 in a past edition)

climate.description: June: roughly 10-25°C (50-77°F) with temperature swings and a real rain/storm chance, typical of early-summer mountain conditions around Bozeman.
challenges: Altitude: start is just under 5,000ft, course climbs above 5,000ft repeatedly

terrain.surface: Mixed gravel and paved roads (course composition varies by year/route length — the 2026 'Waffle' course is ~60% unpaved across 16 sectors). Montana Rockies mountain terrain, technical sections, around Bozeman. Technical rating: 4/5.
features: Good airport access (BZN, Bozeman Yellowstone International, close to town)

biased_opinion.summary: BWR Montana ("Hell of the Bozone") is a Montana Rockies adventure around Bozeman — course length and gain have varied by year (a rider-logged 2025 edition ran ~87mi/~7,900ft; the organizer's stated 2026 'Waffle' course is 115mi/~8,240ft). Founded ~2022. Mixed gravel and paved roads, mountain terrain, technical sections. 500-800 riders. Bozeman, MT (great town, good airport access). June mountain conditions. BWR series entry ($180-$250). If you want Montana Rockies scenery with technical mountain gravel and waffles, this is it. If you need flat terrain, skip it. Mountain terrain (and altitude — the course repeatedly climbs above 5,000ft from a start just under it) is the challenge.
strengths: Good airport access (BZN, close to town)
June mountain weather (variable but generally good)
weaknesses: Elevation (course-year dependent; ~8,000+ ft of gain on recent editions)
Mountain terrain (not for beginners); course climbs above the 5,000ft altitude threshold repeatedly
biased_opinion.bottom_line: BWR Montana is a Montana Rockies mountain adventure based in Bozeman. Distance and gain vary by year — treat any single number as a snapshot, not a fixed course spec. If you want Montana Rockies scenery with mountain gravel, this is it. If you need flat terrain, skip it. Bozeman is a great town with good airport access. BWR series = waffles at finish.

logistics.nearest_airport: Bozeman Yellowstone International (BZN) — close to town
logistics.lodging_note: Bozeman, MT. Book early — race weekend sells out.
logistics.travel_summary: Fly to Bozeman (BZN). Rental car or shuttle. Bozeman lodging — book early. Day-before packet pickup at the Bozeman check-in/expo. Race late June. Post-race waffles.
logistics.food_options: Aid stations on course. Post-race waffles and party.
logistics.camping: Gallatin County Fairgrounds (901 N Black Ave, Bozeman) offers race-weekend RV/camping options — "book early, everything sells out for race weekend."

course_description.character: Course length/gain vary by year — 2026's organizer-stated 'Waffle' course is 115mi/~8,240ft; a rider-logged 2025 edition ran ~87mi/~7,900ft. Mixed gravel and paved roads. Montana Rockies mountain terrain around Bozeman, technical sections. Technical rating: 4/5.

suffering_zones.name: Throughout: Montana Rockies Climbs
suffering_zones.description: Montana Rockies mountain terrain around Bozeman. Cumulative elevation gain in the thousands of feet (varies by year). Technical climbs, with the course repeatedly crossing above 5,000ft.
suffering_zones.survival_strategy: Low gears. Pace conservatively. Technical skills required. 38-42mm tires.

suffering_zones.name: Late-Race: Distance Fatigue
suffering_zones.description: Legs tired in the closing miles. Mountain terrain continues.
suffering_zones.survival_strategy: Break into chunks. Focus on next aid. Mental toughness. Push through.

course_description.signature_challenge: Year-variable distance/elevation (~115mi/~8,240ft per the 2026 organizer figures) + Montana Rockies mountain terrain around Bozeman + repeated crossings above the 5,000ft altitude line.
course_description.ridewithgps_name: BWR Montana 2025 (autotraced) — note: from a prior-year edition; the route is redrawn most years so treat as illustrative, not the current course

history.origin_story: BWR Montana ('Hell of the Bozone') founded ~2022, based in Bozeman, MT. 500-800 riders. Part of the BWR series. Montana Rockies mountain gravel around Bozeman.
notable_moments: 2025: Rider-logged course ran ~87mi/~7,900ft; course composition and length vary by year
history.reputation: BWR series. Montana Rockies, based in Bozeman. Technical mountain terrain. Well-organized. Waffles at finish.

logistics.explanation: MikeMckinney notes check-in and parking were excellent, with $20 camping available at the Gallatin County Fairgrounds and BZN airport minutes from downtown Bozeman. Friday check-in/expo avoids race-morning chaos, though booking early is mandatory—everything sells out for race weekend. (Note: this source's venue name has been corrected to the Bozeman fairgrounds; a prior version of this file misattributed it to a Missoula venue.)

length.explanation: Russell Finsterwald made his winning move after 105 miles on technical singletrack, finishing in 5:03:22 after launching his sprint early. Stella Hobbs "stormed away from the field on the first climb" and rode solo across the line in 5:31:33, showing the 87-mile distance breaks riders at different points. Longer than Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

technicality.explanation: MikeMckinney describes "loose over hard gravel" where "if you got out of the ruts you were surfing around in the soft stuff." The final 4 miles of singletrack was "pretty rocky" with mandatory walking on steep sections, while eric_quiat found 42c tires "pretty perfect" for the varied terrain mix. More technical than most — comparable to Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

elevation.explanation: MikeMckinney logged 7,900 feet of elevation gain with "19 climbs on my head unit" including one punchy 11% section around mile 90. Siena Hermon got a hand-up "at the bottom of a 900-foot climb," learning that accepting feeds before major ascents isn't ideal for heavier riders. More climbing than SBT GRVL at the same score.

climate.explanation: Historical weather data shows average highs of 86°F and lows of 57°F, with a 21% chance of rain on any given day. Flavia Oliveira Parks suffered electronic shifting failures at miles 40 and 97, getting stuck in single gears during the event. The exposed, windy sections demanded strategic racing to avoid "burning too many matches," while altitude and fast gravel conditions created additional challenges beyond pure weather. More weather exposure than Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

altitude.explanation: MikeMckinney warns "Don't forget Bozeman is at 4800 ft, and you ride up to 5500 feet numerous times which you will notice if you don't normally ride that high." Todd Melton observed "The elevation was definitely a challenge for a number of people" during the event. Modest altitude compared to Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

adventure.explanation: MikeMckinney encountered "a few non racer were using the trail riding against the flow" on the public singletrack, while some women's race riders faced an unexpected train stop that added four minutes. The course's exposed, windy sections and loose gravel surfaces demand real bike handling skills beyond pure fitness.

prestige.explanation: Russell Finsterwald praised the course diversity: "I'm a big fan of diverse courses like that where you have to use your technical skills, have to use group dynamics and tactics." Course architect Michael Marckx noted it produced "the closest top five finish in BWR history, as only 24 seconds separated the riders."

race_quality.explanation: Aid stations were "well stocked and appropriately placed, roughly at 22, 39, 67, 98" with "cold coke which was a bonus," reports MikeMckinney. Race organizers handle mechanicals like Flavia's shifting failures professionally, while the 13-hour cutoff accommodates most riders despite challenging terrain.

experience.explanation: Siena Hermon was "smiling ear to ear, descending through grassy fields lined with purple and yellow wildflowers" on the final singletrack. Flavia Oliveira Parks called it "by far the most amazing course they have ever put together" with "unreal" views despite mechanical difficulties.

community.explanation: Race reports note "Locals cheered from porches. Volunteers crushed it" throughout the course. MikeMckinney found "Decent vibes" at the finish with beer and coozies, while Todd Melton is "already planning to do it again" after experiencing Bozeman's hospitality and the event atmosphere.

field_depth.explanation: Todd Melton observed "The level of competition was higher than what I've seen in other events" with many participants having "participated in other BWR events this year." The 350-rider cap creates a competitive field where Russell Finsterwald's technical skills and sprint strength determined victory. Deeper field than most — comparable to Badlands.

value.explanation: $215 standard entry includes waffle breakfast, four well-stocked aid stations, and post-ride amenities, but MikeMckinney notes the finish has "nothing that makes you hang out" beyond beer and a coozy. No cash prizes for a premium-priced gran fondo style event limits the competitive value proposition.

expenses.explanation: Bozeman lodging gets pricey during race weekend, and 'everything sells out' if you don't book early. Direct flights from major hubs into BZN keep travel reasonable, though Bozeman isn't cheap. The $20 camping option at the Gallatin County Fairgrounds helps budget-conscious masochists, but factor in tire replacement after the mud destroys your rubber. Similar expense level to Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

final_verdict.one_liner: The Belgian Waffle Ride that actually lives up to its Belgian difficulty rating, where Montana's mountains separate the climbers from the pretenders.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Show up if you can sustain power above 5,000 feet (the course repeatedly climbs above that line from a Bozeman start just under it) and don't mind walking your bike when the grades hit double digits. This isn't about gravel handling—it's about whether your legs can push a big gear up endless climbs while your lungs adjust to Montana altitude. Skip it if you think 8,000+ feet of elevation gain (the current organizer-stated figure; it has varied by year) sounds fun on paper.
final_verdict.alternatives: Leadville Trail 100 MTB gives you the same altitude suffering with actual prize money, while Unbound keeps you closer to sea level if you prefer speed over elevation. For pure climbing masochism without the gravel pretense, just ride Mount Washington Hillclimb and save yourself 100+ miles of buildup.

primary.name: Maxxis Reaver
primary.why: Technical all-rounder. EXO+ casing shrugs off rocks and roots.

videos.title: Big Sky, Big Wins: How Belgian Waffle Ride Montana Delivered a Double Victory
videos.description:
Welcome to the very first Belgian Waffle Ride Montana! We're in Bozeman, ready to explore what "Big Sky Country" truly means for gravel racing. I head into the race with the BWR Quad Crown series lead… can I hold onto it? With 5+ miles of singletrack in the final stretch, this race is far from over at 8 miles to go. Get ready for Big Sky Country gravel and an epic finish!

Special thanks to: 
@Bikeflights 
$10 off next shipment through July 31 (Limit one use per user)
Use coupon code: MONTANA10
https://bit.ly/FinstyBWRMont

@riserecords :
Winona Fighter - You Look Like A Drunk Phoebe Bridgers 
Alkaline Trio - At Sickness
Chuck Ragan - Reel My Heart 
Dance Gavin Dance - War Machine
Memphis May Fire - The Other Side

@KDubzDidWhat | Filming X Editing

videos.curation_reason: First-person race recap from BWR Montana with excellent view count and detailed course insights about the unique singletrack finish.

videos.title: BWR Montana 2025 - QUADRUPLE CROWN Ep. 4
videos.description:
Welcome to the 4th and final stop of the 2025 Belgian Waffle Ride Quadruple Crown and Inaugural event in Bozeman Montana!

Coming off a win at the 3rd stop in his home state of Utah, Joe Goettl looks to back that up with a good result here. This is his final shot at making his original goal of an overall series podium.

Thank you to everyone who's watched all the previous episodes and we really hope you'll enjoy this finale! 

📈 Strava File: https://www.strava.com/activities/14956431695

videos.curation_reason: Professional race documentation with detailed pre-race course analysis and technical setup insights specific to BWR Montana.

quotes.text: With 5+ miles of singletrack in the final stretch, this race is far from over at 8 miles to go. Four may enter, but who knows how many will come out. It's a lot of corners to blow.

I think for a BWR, I was expecting it to be a little bit rougher and gnarlier, but so far it's definitely looking to be like a fast kind of road racy style race, but there are a ton of rollers, so it's definitely not going to be an easy race.

I look to Belgian waffle rides as events that really bring the best courses that we race on all season. They're the most encompassing of everything that I think gravel racing should be. Like you have a lot of single track out there, just challenging unroad sectors that pop up out of nowhere.

terrain_notes.text: The course includes a lot of single track sections that pop up throughout the race, creating technical challenges 100 miles into the event when riders are fatigued.

gear_mentions.text: Most riders opted for 45mm tires. One rider ran Teras 45s and discussed whether to use J1 RS at 45mm width versus other 45mm options.

race_day_tips.text: The race will likely break up into small groups all day and probably be down to three or four riders going into the single track sections.

additional_quotes.text: I look to Belgian waffle rides as events that really bring the best courses that we race on all season. They're the most encompassing of everything that I think gravel racing should be. Like you have a lot of single track out there, just challenging unroad sectors that pop up out of nowhere.
Having gotten second at BWR Arizona and then having a decent ride in BWR California getting seventh, I come into the weekend with the lead in the series which is pretty cool. This is the final stop.

rider_intel.search_text: BWR Montana serves as the final stop in the Belgian Waffle Ride Triple Crown series, taking place in Big Sky Country around Bozeman, Montana. Riders describe the course as encompassing everything gravel racing should be, featuring significant single track sections that create technical challenges, especially when riders hit them 100 miles into the race while fatigued. The terrain includes challenging unroad sectors that pop up unexpectedly throughout the course. Most competitive riders opt for 45mm tires to handle the varied terrain. The race typically breaks down into small groups throughout the day, with expectations that only three or four riders will remain together heading into the technical single track sections. The event attracts strong competition as riders vie for both individual race wins and overall series standings.
